title: Your First SQL Query
description: Time to write some actual SQL code!

slides.title: The SELECT Statement 🎯
slides.content:
# The Holy Grail of SQL

If you only remember one word in SQL, let it be **`SELECT`**.

`SELECT` is how you ask the database to retrieve data for you. It's the equivalent of saying "Hey database, GET me this information..."

slides.title: Anatomy of a Query 🫀
slides.content:
## A Basic Query

A basic SELECT statement has two parts:

1. `SELECT [what you want]`
2. `FROM [which table]`

For example: `SELECT name FROM users;` asks the database to get only the names of all users.

quizData.question: If we want to retrieve the `email` column from the `users` table, how would we start writing that query?
quizData.explanation: Always use SELECT when retrieving data. The correct format is SELECT [column] FROM [table].

slides.title: The Magic Star ⭐
slides.content:
## Give me everything!

Often, you don't want just one column; you want *every* column in the table.

Instead of typing `SELECT name, email, age, gender, address FROM users;`, you can use the magic star symbol **`*`** (asterisk).

`SELECT * FROM users;` literally translates to "Select EVERYTHING from the users table."

quizData.question: What does the asterisk (*) symbol do in a SELECT query?

options.text: It selects all rows but deletes the table
It selects ALL columns in the table

quizData.explanation: In SQL, the asterisk (*) is a wildcard that means 'every single column'.

slides.title: See it in Action
slides.content: Write your first query! Use the magic star to retrieve all columns from the `products` table.

slides.title: Don't forget the semicolon ;
slides.content: Notice how all our queries end with a semicolon `;`? In SQL, the semicolon tells the database that you have finished your thought!
